codeQualityIssues.description: Rng struct (xorshift64) is duplicated across 5+ test files (integration_stress, stress_buddy_coalescing, scheduler_state, stress_sched_ctx_concurrent, stress_alloc_fragmentation). Identical implementation in each. A shared test utility module could eliminate this duplication, though the current test architecture convention (standalone files with #[path] imports) makes this an accepted pattern.

ChannelTable and TimerTable models are substantially duplicated from adversarial_churn.rs, with integration_stress adding waiter registration/unregistration methods. If models evolve (e.g., kernel channel.rs changes), both files need updating. Low risk since these models are stable and the test architecture favors standalone files.

HeapModel is a simplified tracker (Vec of address/size pairs) rather than a faithful replica of the kernel's linked-list + slab allocator. This is acceptable for integration testing (goal is cross-subsystem interaction, not individual allocator correctness), but worth noting that it won't catch allocator-specific bugs in integration scenarios.

ModelThread.complete_wait_for() clears stale_waiters before populating new ones (line ~370). If called twice without an intervening take_stale_waiters(), previous stale entries are silently lost. All current tests handle this correctly, but the API doesn't enforce the required call sequence. A debug_assert on stale_waiters.is_empty() at the top of complete_wait_for would catch misuse.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The Rng (xorshift64) struct is now duplicated across 5+ test files. If the test suite continues to grow, a shared test utilities file documenting this pattern (or extracting it) would reduce maintenance burden. Currently follows established convention but approaching a threshold where extraction would pay off.

Host-side kernel model structs (ChannelTable, TimerTable, SchedContextTable, ProcessTable, ModelThread, HeapModel) are now used in multiple test files (adversarial_churn.rs, integration_stress.rs, and others). The integration_stress models extend the adversarial_churn models with waiter registration. No documentation captures which test file has the most complete/authoritative model of each kernel subsystem.

summary: 12 integration stress tests in a single 1613-line file, covering all 3 required areas: (1) multi-subsystem stress with 1500+ randomized IPC+scheduling+allocation ops across 8 simulated threads (VAL-INTEG-001), (2) process lifecycle churn with 75 create/kill cycles including handle transfer and 4 variants (VAL-INTEG-002), (3) stale waiter cleanup with basic, mixed-type, timeout, 50-round repeated, and no-spurious-wakeup tests (VAL-INTEG-005). Code follows established test architecture patterns: host-side kernel models, #[path] imports for shared types, seeded deterministic PRNG, comprehensive cleanup+leak assertions. All 1336 tests pass (1324 baseline + 12 new). No blocking issues found. Non-blocking observations: model struct duplication across test files is growing but follows accepted convention; HeapModel is simplified (acceptable for integration scope). Worker followed kernel-auditor skill procedure correctly.
